title: Setting Up Your Profile
content:
Setting Up Your Profile
Table of Contents
Getting to your settings
Profile Information
Teaching Profile
Account Information
Appearance
What other members see
Getting to your settings
Click Settings in the main navigation. The settings sidebar opens on the left. Click General to reach your profile settings. The sidebar can be collapsed using the arrow icon at the top if you need more space.
Profile Information
The Profile Information section is the foundation of your public presence on GOYA.
Name and tagline
Fill in your First Name and Last Name. These appear on your member profile and anywhere your content is attributed.
The Introduction field (up to 120 characters) is a short tagline shown beneath your name — for example, "Hatha teacher and wellness coach in Berlin." Keep it concise and descriptive.
Video Introduction
Paste a YouTube URL in the Video Introduction field to embed a short welcome video on your profile. This is optional but helps community members get to know you.
Bio
The Bio field (up to 500 characters) gives you space to describe your background, approach, and what you offer the community. Write in the first person and keep it welcoming.
Location
Enter your Location in "City, Country" format. This is visible on your public profile.
Social links
Connect your online presence using the Website, Instagram, and YouTube fields. Enter your full website URL, your Instagram handle (with or without the @ symbol), and your YouTube channel name.
Click Save Changes when you are done with this section.
Teaching Profile
If your role is Teacher, you will see a Teaching Profile section below the main profile form. This section does not appear for students.
Fill in the following fields to help other members understand your background:
Years Teaching — select a range from the dropdown (e.g. "510 years")
Teaching Format — choose InPerson, Online, or Hybrid
Influences — select what has shaped your practice
Teaching Styles — choose the styles you teach (e.g. Hatha Yoga, Yin Yoga, Vinyasa Yoga)
Teaching Focus — select your primary areas of focus (e.g. Breath Work, Flexibility and Mobility)
Lineage — select up to 3 lineages that inform your teaching
Changes save along with the main profile when you click Save Changes.
School owners see a School Profile section instead, with fields for programs offered, year established, and course delivery format.
Account Information
The Account Information section shows readonly details about your membership:
Member Number (MRN) — your unique GOYA member reference number
Member Since — the month and year you joined
Role — your current role (e.g. Teacher, Wellness Practitioner)
You can update your Email Address here. Click Update after changing it — a verification email will be sent to your new address before the change takes effect.
Appearance
The Appearance section lets you choose your preferred color theme for the GOYA interface. Select the theme you prefer and it will apply immediately.
What other members see
Your public member profile displays:
Your name and Introduction tagline
Your avatar (initials shown until avatar upload is available)
Your Bio
Your Location
Your Teaching Profile details (styles, focus, lineage, years teaching)
Links to your Website, Instagram, and YouTube
Your email address, Member Number, and account settings are private and never shown to other members.
See also
Teacher Role Overview
Credits and Hours

title: Register Your School on GOYA
content:
Register Your School on GOYA
If you own or operate a yoga school, you can list it on GOYA to manage designations, connect with students, and grow your school's presence on the platform.
Where to Find the Registration Link
As a teacher without a registered school, you will see a Register Your School prompt in three places:
Dashboard — right sidebar widget titled "Own a Yoga School?"
Settings > Subscriptions — callout below your membership card
AddOns & Upgrades — banner above the product grid
Each prompt includes a Register Your School button that takes you to /schools/create.
Who Sees These Prompts
Teachers without a school — all three prompts are visible
Teachers who already own a school — prompts are hidden
Students and wellness practitioners — prompts are not shown
Registration Wizard
The registration flow is a 3step guided wizard:
Step 1: School Name
Enter your school name (minimum 3 characters). A URL slug is generated automatically — for example, "Sunrise Yoga Academy" becomes goya.org/schools/sunriseyogaacademy. You can edit the slug directly. A green checkmark confirms the URL is available; a red X means it is already taken.
Step 2: Choose Your Designations
Select one or more GOYA school designations your school will offer:
 Full Name 
 GOYA Certified Yoga School 200  GOYACYS300 
 GOYA Certified Yoga School 500  GOYACCYS 
 GOYA Certified Prenatal Yoga School  GOYACYYS 
 GOYA Certified Restorative Yoga School  GOYACMS 
Pricing per designation:
EUR 40.00/year (annual subscription, renewable)
EUR 99.00 onetime signup fee
A running total at the bottom updates as you select. You can add more designations later — you don't need to select everything now.
Step 3: Payment
Click Continue to Payment to proceed to Stripe Checkout. Your school details and selections are saved as a draft in your browser, so you can safely navigate back if needed.
After successful payment, you will be redirected to your school's onboarding flow to complete your profile.
Your progress is saved
If you close your browser midregistration or Stripe returns you to the previous page, your school name, slug, and selected designations are restored automatically from your browser's local storage.

title: Upgrading to Teacher
content:
Upgrading to Teacher
Table of Contents
Who can upgrade
What changes after approval
The upgrade process
Step 1 — Review the benefits and pricing
Step 2 — Upload your certificates
Step 3 — Authorize payment
After you submit
Common questions
Who can upgrade
Any GOYA member with a Student or Wellness Practitioner role can apply to upgrade to GOYA Certified Teacher. The upgrade requires valid teaching credentials and a yearly membership fee.
What changes after approval
Once your application is approved your account role changes to Teacher. You will gain access to:
My Events — submit workshops, trainings, and other events to the community calendar
My Courses — submit courses to the GOYA Academy
Teaching Hours — log and track your active teaching time
Teaching Profile — display your teaching styles, lineage, and experience on your member profile
Exclusive teacher resources and materials
Your credentials displayed on your public GOYA profile
Connection to the broader GOYA teacher community
The upgrade process
Go to /upgrade to begin. The process has three steps.
Step 1 — Review the benefits and pricing
The first screen shows what you gain with a teacher membership and the annual fee:
Teacher Membership — $39.00 / year
Read through the listed benefits. When you are ready, click Start Upgrade to move to the next step.
Step 2 — Upload your certificates
Upload between 1 and 3 certificate files that verify your teaching qualifications. Accepted file types are PDF, JPG, PNG, and WEBP, with a maximum size of 4 MB per file.
To add a file:
Click Add Certificate.
Select your file from your device. The file uploads immediately and appears in the list.
Repeat for additional certificates (up to 3 total).
To remove a file that you added, click Remove next to the file name.
Once at least one certificate is uploaded, the Continue to Payment button becomes active. Click it to proceed.
Click Back at any time to return to the previous step without losing what you have already uploaded.
Step 3 — Authorize payment
The final step confirms the payment details:
Teacher Membership — $39.00 / year
Note: Your payment is not charged at this point. The charge only occurs after the GOYA team has verified your credentials.
The screen also confirms how many certificate files are ready to send.
Click Proceed to Checkout to be redirected to the secure Stripe payment page. Complete payment on the Stripe page to finalize your submission.
After you submit
After completing checkout, you land on a confirmation page with the message:
> "Your upgrade request has been submitted! Our team will verify your credentials within 48 hours."
What happens next:
The GOYA team reviews your certificates within 48 hours.
If approved, your role changes to Teacher, your teacher features become available, and your annual membership is charged.
You will receive a notification once the review is complete.
If there is an issue with your credentials, the team will reach out to let you know.
Common questions
Can I edit my certificates after submitting?
No. Once you complete checkout, the submission is final. If you need to provide different certificates, contact GOYA support.
What if my application is not approved?
The team will contact you. Your payment is not charged if your credentials are not verified.
Can I cancel my teacher membership?
Yes. Go to Settings > General and use the End Subscription option in the Danger Zone. Your teacher access continues until the end of the current billing period.
See also
Teacher Role Overview
Submitting Events
Submitting Courses

title: Academy Guide
content:
Academy Guide
The GOYA Academy is the platform's course library — a growing collection of videobased content from the GOYA team and certified community members. This guide explains how to browse, enrol, and track your progress.
Table of Contents
Opening the Academy
GOYA courses vs Member courses
Course categories and levels
Browsing and filtering courses
Reading a course card
The course overview page
Enrolling in a course
Tracking your progress
Free vs Members Only courses
Opening the Academy
Click Academy in the main navigation. The page opens with the full course library displayed as a grid of cards.
GOYA courses vs Member courses
Like events, every course in the Academy belongs to one of two types:
GOYA Courses — Content produced or curated by the GOYA team. These courses meet GOYA's quality and content standards.
Member Courses — Courses submitted by certified teachers and schools. Member submissions go through a review and approval process before they appear in the library.
Use the Type filter row beneath the category bar to toggle between All Courses, GOYA Courses, and Member Courses.
Course categories and levels
Categories available in the Academy:
 What it covers 
 Structured skillbuilding and instructional sessions  Yoga Sequence 
 Philosophy, traditions, and contemplative teachings  Music Playlist 
 Academic content, studies, and evidencebased resources 
Difficulty levels are shown on each course card:
Beginner — shown in green
Intermediate — shown in amber
Advanced — shown in rose/red
All Levels — suitable for any practitioner
Browsing and filtering courses
The filter bar sits just below the page hero and stays visible as you scroll.
Filter by category — Click any category button in the top row (All Courses, Workshop, Yoga Sequence, Dharma Talk, Music Playlist, Research). The course count in the top right updates to reflect your selection.
Filter by type — Use the Type: row to switch between All Courses, GOYA Courses, and Member Courses.
Filter by progress — If you are logged in, a third row appears: My Progress. Use it to quickly find:
My Courses — every course you have enrolled in
In Progress — courses you have started but not yet finished
Completed — courses you have marked as done
All three filter rows work together. Combine them to find, for example, all Beginner Yoga Sequences you have not yet started.
Reading a course card
Each card in the grid shows:
Thumbnail — a colour gradient with a category icon; completed courses show a green Completed badge in the top left
Access badge — Free (green) or Members Only (dark) in the top right
Category badge — shown at the bottom of the thumbnail
Title and instructor name
Short description
Duration and level in the card footer
Progress bar — visible on cards for courses you have started or completed
The course overview page
Click any course card to open its detail page. Here you will find:
Full course description and what the course covers
Instructor information
Duration, level, and category
An enrolment card with the Start Course (or Continue / Completed) button
Enrolling in a course
On the course overview page, click Start Course (or the equivalent enrolment button). This records your enrolment and marks the course as In Progress in your library.
You will be taken to the course lesson immediately. The course content is videobased — watch it in the player on screen.
If you leave before finishing, your progress is saved. Return to the Academy, filter by In Progress, and click the course to pick up where you left off.
Tracking your progress
Your progress across all enrolled courses is tracked automatically. You can see it in two places:
On the Academy page — Enrolled courses show a progress bar at the bottom of their card:
A partial bar indicates In Progress
A full bar (100%) confirms Completed
Using the My Progress filters — Switch to My Courses, In Progress, or Completed in the filter bar to see a focused view of your learning history.
When you finish a course, mark it as complete using the button on the course page. The card badge updates to Completed and the course moves to your Completed list.
Free vs Members Only courses
Courses display one of two access levels:
Free — Available to all loggedin GOYA members at no cost.
Members Only — Requires an active GOYA membership subscription. If you see a Members Only course you want to access, go to Settings > Subscriptions to view available membership plans.
See also
Events Guide
Getting Started
Student Role Overview

title: Events Guide
content:
Events Guide
The GOYA events calendar is where the community shares live learning opportunities — from local workshops to international conferences. This guide explains how to find events, understand the different event types, and register.
Table of Contents
Opening the events page
GOYA events vs Member events
Event categories
Browsing and filtering events
Reading an event listing
Viewing an event detail page
Registering for an event
Opening the events page
Click Events in the main navigation. The page shows all upcoming published events in chronological order.
GOYA events vs Member events
Every event on GOYA belongs to one of two types:
GOYA Events — Organised or curated directly by the GOYA team. These are highlighted with a blue indicator in the Event Type filter.
Member Events — Submitted by certified teachers and schools in the GOYA community. Member events go through a review before they are published. These appear with an indigo indicator.
You can switch between them using the Event Type filter:
All Events — shows both GOYA and Member events (the default)
GOYA — shows only GOYAorganised events
Member — shows only communitysubmitted events
On desktop, the type filter is in the left sidebar. On mobile, it appears as a segmented control at the top of the page.
Event categories
Events are organised into the following categories:
 What it covers 
 Handson skillbuilding sessions  Teacher Training 
 Philosophy, teachings, and contemplative discussions  Conference 
 Sequencefocused practice sessions  Music Playlist 
 Academic and evidencebased presentations 
Each event card and detail page shows a coloured category badge so you can identify the type at a glance.
Browsing and filtering events
Filter by date — On desktop, use the mini calendar in the left sidebar. Days that have events are shown with a small dot beneath the date. Click a highlighted date to filter the list to that day only. Click Clear date filter to reset.
On mobile, tap the date button in the filter bar at the top to open the date picker sheet. Tap Done when finished.
Filter by category — On desktop, click any category in the Category section of the left sidebar. On mobile, tap the filter button (the funnel icon) in the filter bar to open the category sheet.
Filter by type — Use the Event Type filter (desktop sidebar or mobile segmented bar) to switch between All, GOYA, and Member events.
Combining filters — All three filters work together. For example, you can show only GOYA Workshops on a specific date. A Clear filters button appears in the header row whenever any filter is active.
Reading an event listing
Each event card in the list shows:
Date block — the day and month on the lefthand side
Category badge and format badge (e.g., InPerson, Online)
Title and short description
Time — start and end time
Location — venue or city (for inperson events)
Instructor — the teacher or facilitator running the event
Spots remaining — shown in amber when fewer than 10 spots are left
Price — either a dollar amount or a green Free badge
Click anywhere on the card to open the full event detail page.
Viewing an event detail page
The event detail page gives you the complete picture:
Hero section — event title, category, format, and date
About This Event — full description from the organiser
Date & Time — exact start and end times
Location — full address or online link (if provided by the organiser)
Instructor — name of the person leading the event
Price and registration — a booking card on the right side (desktop) or at the bottom (mobile)
A Back to Events link at the top of the page takes you back to the calendar without losing your filter settings.
Registering for an event
Registration details and links are provided by each event's organiser. When an event has external registration, you will see a registration button or link on the event detail page — clicking it will take you to the organiser's booking page.
For free events, the event detail page may include direct contact information or instructions from the organiser.
If an event shows spots remaining, register early to secure your place. The spots counter updates in real time.
Past events (where the date has already passed) are still accessible in the directory and display a Past Event badge, but registration is no longer available.
See also
Academy Guide
Finding Teachers
Getting Started

title: Finding Teachers
content:
Finding Teachers
The GOYA member directory is where you can discover certified teachers, wellness practitioners, and yoga schools from around the world. This guide explains how to search, filter, and connect.
Table of Contents
Opening the directory
Browsing the list view
Using the map view
Filtering by role, country, and more
Viewing a teacher profile
Connecting with a teacher
Understanding connection types
Opening the directory
Click Members in the main navigation bar. The directory loads with all active GOYA members visible as a scrollable list on the left.
Browsing the list view
Each member card in the list shows:
Name and avatar
Location (city and country)
Role badge — Teacher, Student, School, or Wellness Practitioner
Designations — any certifications or specialties the member has listed
A short bio excerpt
Click any card to highlight it on the map and open a preview. Click View Profile (or click directly to the profile URL) to see the full member page.
Using the map view
The map displays all members who have added a city and country to their profile. It is especially useful when you want to find a teacher near you or in a specific region.
Pan and zoom the map to explore different areas
Click a pin to see that member's card in the list — it will scroll into view and be highlighted
Clicking the highlighted card opens their profile
The map and list stay in sync — selecting a card on the left moves the map to centre on that member.
Filtering by role, country, and more
Use the sidebar filters (desktop) or the filter panel (mobile) to narrow down the directory.
Role filter — Click one of the role chips at the top to show only members with that role:
All — everyone in the directory
Teacher — certified yoga teachers
Student — student practitioners
School — yoga schools and studios
Wellness Practitioner — practitioners outside traditional teacher roles
Country filter — Click the Country dropdown to select one or more countries. You can type to search for a specific country. Selected countries appear as removable tags below the dropdown.
Additional chip filters (designations, teaching styles, etc.) may appear in the sidebar depending on the member data available. Click any chip to toggle it on or off; click again to remove it.
To reset all filters, remove your selections individually or refresh the page.
Viewing a teacher profile
Click any member card to open their full profile page. A teacher's profile includes:
Name, role badge, and location
About section — their bio and background
Connect section — links to their website, Instagram, and YouTube (if provided)
Connections — mutual connections you share, if any
Teachers who have filled in their Teaching Profile also display details such as teaching style, lineage, years of experience, and the types of programs they offer.
Connecting with a teacher
On a teacher's profile page, you will see an action button in the right column. The label changes based on your role and theirs:
Student visiting a Teacher profile — the button reads Request Mentorship. Clicking it sends a mentorship request to that teacher.
Student visiting another Student's profile — the button reads Connect, sending a peer connection request.
Student visiting a School profile — no connection button appears (schools accept faculty applications separately).
After you send a request the button changes to Mentorship Requested (or Request Sent for peer connections) and becomes inactive until the other member responds.
Understanding connection types
GOYA has two types of connections available to students:
Peer connection — A mutual connection between two members of any role. Both members can see each other in their Connections list in Settings.
Mentorship — A directed relationship from student to teacher. The teacher receives your request in their Inbox and can accept or decline. An accepted mentorship appears on both profiles under Connections.
You can manage all your existing connections from Settings > Connections.
See also
Getting Started
Student Role Overview

title: Getting Started
content:
Getting Started
Welcome to GOYA. This guide walks you through the first things to do after creating your account, so you can get the most out of the platform right away.
Table of Contents
Step 1 — Complete your profile
Step 2 — Find teachers and members
Step 3 — Explore events
Step 4 — Browse the Academy
Step 5 — Get to know your Settings
Step 1 — Complete your profile
A complete profile helps other members find you and understand your background. Head to Settings from the navigation menu, then select General.
Things to fill in:
First name and Last name — these appear on your public member card
Avatar — upload a profile photo so others can recognise you
Bio — a short description of your yoga journey, practice, or interests
Location — your city and country; this powers the map view in the member directory
Social links — Instagram, YouTube, and website links appear on your public profile
Click Save Changes when you are done. Your profile is visible to other members in the directory immediately.
Step 2 — Find teachers and members
Once your profile is set up, explore the community. Go to Members in the main navigation.
The directory shows every GOYA member with their role, location, and designations. You can:
Filter by role — Teacher, Student, School, or Wellness Practitioner
Filter by country using the country dropdown
Switch to the Map view to browse members geographically
Click any member card to view their full profile
If you find a teacher you want to learn from, you can send them a Request Mentorship from their profile page.
See Finding Teachers for the full guide.
Step 3 — Explore events
Go to Events in the main navigation to see the full community calendar.
Events include workshops, teacher trainings, dharma talks, conferences, and more. You can filter by:
Date — pick a specific day using the mini calendar on the left (desktop) or the date picker at the top (mobile)
Category — narrow down by type, such as Workshop or Dharma Talk
Event Type — choose between GOYA Events (organised by the GOYA team) and Member Events (submitted by teachers and schools)
Click any event to view its full details including time, location, instructor, and price.
See Events Guide for the full guide.
Step 4 — Browse the Academy
Go to Academy in the navigation to access the GOYA course library. Courses are videobased and cover a range of topics: yoga sequences, dharma talks, music playlists, workshops, and research.
Use the filter bar at the top to browse by:
Category — such as Workshop, Yoga Sequence, or Dharma Talk
Type — GOYA Courses (curated by GOYA) or Member Courses (submitted by teachers)
Click any course card to view its overview page. From there you can enrol and start learning.
See Academy Guide for the full guide.
Step 5 — Get to know your Settings
Your Settings page is the central hub for managing your account. Access it from the navigation menu. You will find:
General — edit your profile information, bio, avatar, social links, and appearance preferences
Subscriptions — manage your GOYA membership plan and billing
Connections — view and manage your network of connections
Inbox — check notifications and messages from other members
Help — contact support or ask the AI assistant a question
See also
Finding Teachers
Events Guide
Academy Guide
Student Role Overview

title: Student Role Overview
content:
Student Role Overview
Welcome to GOYA — a global community for yoga practitioners, teachers, and schools. As a student member, you are part of a network built to deepen your practice and connect you with qualified teachers around the world.
Table of Contents
What students can do
How the platform is organised
Credits and hours
Upgrading to teacher
Next steps
What students can do
 Available to students 
 Yes  Browse the member directory 
 Yes  Connect with other members (peer connection) 
 Yes  Browse the event calendar 
 Yes  Browse the GOYA Academy course library 
 Yes  Track CE credits, karma hours, and practice hours 
 No (teacher role required)  Submit courses for publication 
 No (teacher role required) 
How the platform is organised
Members — Browse and connect with the global GOYA community. Find teachers by location, specialty, or designation using the interactive directory and map view.
Events — Discover upcoming workshops, teacher trainings, dharma talks, conferences, and more. Events are listed on a calendar with date, category, and type filters.
Academy — The GOYA course library. Browse and enrol in videobased courses covering yoga sequences, dharma talks, music playlists, workshops, and research content.
Settings — Manage your profile, subscriptions, connections, inbox, and credits all in one place. Access your settings from the navigation menu at any time.
Credits — Track your continuing education (CE) credits, karma hours, practice hours, and community contributions at /credits.
Credits and hours
Even as a student, you can log and track your hours on the platform. GOYA recognises several types of contributions:
CE Credits — Continuing education activities
Karma Hours — Service and community contributions
Practice Hours — Your personal yoga practice
Community Credits — Participation in the broader GOYA community
Visit My Credits & Hours (/credits) to submit and track your progress.
Upgrading to teacher
If you hold a yoga teaching certification and want to share your work with the GOYA community, you can apply to upgrade your account to the Teacher role. Teachers can submit events, publish courses, and log Teaching Hours.
See Upgrade to Teacher for details.
Next steps
Getting started — first steps after signup
Finding teachers in the directory
Browsing and registering for events
Browsing and enrolling in courses

title: Upgrading to Teacher
content:
Upgrading to Teacher
If you hold a yoga teaching certification and want to contribute to the GOYA community as a teacher, you can apply to upgrade your account from Student to Teacher. This page gives you a brief overview of what changes, what is required, and how the process works.
Table of Contents
What changes with the Teacher role
Requirements
The upgrade process
After the upgrade
What changes with the Teacher role
Upgrading to Teacher unlocks a set of features that are not available to students:
Submit events — Share your workshops, teacher trainings, dharma talks, and other offerings on the GOYA community calendar. Submitted events go through a review before they are published.
Submit courses — Publish videobased courses to the GOYA Academy. Like events, course submissions are reviewed before appearing in the library.
Log Teaching Hours — Track the hours you spend actively teaching classes, workshops, private sessions, and intensives. Teaching Hours are a separate credit type from the CE, karma, and practice hours available to all members.
Teaching Profile — Your settings page gains a full Teaching Profile section where you can document your teaching style, lineage, years of experience, teaching focus areas, and program types. This information appears on your public member profile and makes it easier for students to find you.
The table below summarises the difference:
 Student 
 Yes 
 Yes 
 Yes 
 No 
 No 
 No 
 No 
Requirements
To upgrade to the Teacher role you need:
A valid yoga teaching certificate — GOYA requires documentary proof of a recognised certification before granting the Teacher role.
An active GOYA account — You must be signed in to apply.
The specific certificates and training programs recognised by GOYA are listed in the full upgrade guide linked below.
The upgrade process
The upgrade is handled through your account settings. In brief:
Go to Settings from the navigation menu.
Locate the Upgrade to Teacher section (visible to Student accounts).
Follow the steps to submit your certificate details and any required documentation.
The GOYA team reviews your application and will notify you via your Inbox when it is approved or if further information is needed.
For stepbystep instructions, screenshots, and answers to common questions, see the dedicated guide:
Full Upgrade to Teacher Guide
After the upgrade
Once your application is approved your role badge changes to Teacher immediately. You will see new sections appear in your settings sidebar:
My Events — create and manage your event submissions
My Courses — create and manage your course submissions
Teaching Hours — log your active teaching time
Your public member profile is also updated automatically to reflect the Teacher role and any Teaching Profile information you add.
See also
Student Role Overview
Getting Started
Teacher Role Overview

title: API Reference
content:
API Reference
GOYA v2 exposes a versioned REST API at /api/v1/ for external integrations. All endpoints require an API key.
Table of Contents
Authentication
Rate Limiting
Response Format
Permissions
Route Categories
Full Endpoint Reference
Authentication
API requests must include an API key in one of two ways:
Header (preferred):
``
xapikey: goya_your_key_here
`
Authorization header fallback:
`
Authorization: Bearer goya_your_key_here
`
Keys are stored in the api_keys table as SHA256 hashes. The raw key is only shown once at creation time. Keys are managed from the admin panel at /admin/apikeys.
Rate Limiting
100 requests per 60second window per API key, enforced inmemory per server instance.
When the limit is exceeded, the API returns:
`json
HTTP 429 Too Many Requests
RetryAfter: 42
{
  "success": false,
  "error": {
    "code": "RATE_LIMITED",
    "message": "Rate limit exceeded. Max 100 requests per minute."
  },
  "meta": {
    "retry_after": 42
  }
}
`
Response Format
All responses follow a consistent envelope:
Success:
`json
{
  "success": true,
  "data": { ... }
}
`
Error:
`json
{
  "success": false,
  "error": {
    "code": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
    "message": "Humanreadable description"
  }
}
`
Common error codes:

Permissions
Each API key has a permissions array. Valid values:
 Access

 Supersedes all other permissions  'write') explicitly. The admin permission bypasses all permission checks.

title: Architecture
content:
Architecture Table of Contents Folder Tree app/ — Pages and API Routes lib/ — ServerSide Utilities supabase/ — Database Key Patterns Server Components by Default Server Actions for Mutations PageContainer Layout Standard RoleBased Routing AdminShell and SettingsShell Folder Tree `` GOYA v2/ ├── app/ # Next.js App Router root │ ├── layout.tsx # Root layout (fonts, providers, analytics) │ ├── page.tsx # Homepage (redirects authed users → /dashboard) │ ├── globals.css # Tailwind base styles │ │ │ ├── admin/ # Admin section (rolegated: admin Module supabase.ts Server Supabase client for Server Components and Server Actions supabase/service.ts Lazy singleton Stripe instance (imports serveronly) email/send.ts validateApiKey(), rateLimit(), requirePermission() impersonation.ts Append rows to audit_log table secrets/ supabase/ — Database All schema changes live in supabase/migrations/ as numbered SQL files. There is no ORM — queries use the Supabase JS client with the generated types/supabase.ts types for type safety. After adding or pulling a migration file, run: `bash npx supabase db push ` Key Patterns Server Components by Default Every page.tsx and layout.tsx is a React Server Component unless it begins with 'use client'. Data fetching happens inline using await supabase.from(...).select(...) — no useEffect, no loading states for initial data. `tsx // app/dashboard/page.tsx — Server Component import { createSupabaseServerClient } from '@/lib/supabaseServer' export default async function DashboardPage() { const supabase = await createSupabaseServerClient() const { data: profile } = await supabase.from('profiles').select('*').single() return
{profile?.full_name}
} ` Client Components are added at the leaf level for interactive UI (forms, modals, charts). Server Actions for Mutations All create/update/delete operations use Server Actions, not clientside API calls. Server Actions are defined in actions.ts files colocated with their page or in lib/. `tsx // app/settings/actions.ts 'use server' import { createSupabaseServerClient } from '@/lib/supabaseServer' export async function updateProfile(formData: FormData) { const supabase = await createSupabaseServerClient() // ... mutation } ` PageContainer Layout Standard Every page content section wraps its content in PageContainer to enforce the maxw7xl (1280px) width standard. `tsx import PageContainer from '@/app/components/ui/PageContainer' // Fullbleed background with contained content:

Content here
` Never hardcode maxwidth, mxauto, or horizontal padding directly on page elements. RoleBased Routing Roles are stored in profiles.role. Valid values: student, teacher, wellness_practitioner, moderator, admin. Access control happens at two layers: Middleware (middleware.ts): Blocks unauthenticated users from protected paths. Enforces maintenance mode. Validates impersonation cookie integrity. Layoutlevel server check: app/admin/layout.tsx reverifies the role serverside and redirects if insufficient. This is the authoritative gate — middleware alone is not sufficient because it runs on Edge (no full DB access). AdminShell and SettingsShell AdminShell and SettingsShell are serverrendered sidebar layout components that receive children from the active page. app/admin/layout.tsx verifies role, then renders {children} app/settings/layout.tsx verifies auth, then renders {children} To add a new admin section: create the page under app/admin/yoursection/page.tsx, then add the nav link inside AdminShell.tsx`. See Also databaseschema.md — Full table reference authentication.md — Auth flow and role enforcement details contributing.md — How to add new pages

title: Authentication
content:
Authentication
GOYA v2 uses Supabase Auth for all session management. Authentication state is stored in cookies (not localStorage) via the @supabase/ssr package, making it available to both Server Components and middleware.
Table of Contents
Supabase Auth Flow
Supabase Client Variants
Session Management in Middleware
Role System
Checking Roles in Code
Admin Impersonation
Migrated Users — Password Reset Gate
Maintenance Mode
Supabase Auth Flow
User submits credentials on /signin or /register.
Supabase issues a JWT access token + refresh token, stored as HTTPonly cookies (sb*).
The @supabase/ssr browser client (lib/supabase.ts) reads these cookies automatically.
On each request, middleware calls supabase.auth.getUser() which validates the token (and silently refreshes it via setAll cookie callback).
Downstream Server Components call createSupabaseServerClient() which reads the same cookies from the request.
Password reset for invited or migrated members goes through /forgotpassword → /resetpassword (Supabase magic link flow).
Supabase Client Variants
Three clients exist for different contexts:
 When to use 
 Client Components — createBrowserClient, session in cookies  lib/supabaseServer.ts 
 Service role — bypasses RLS entirely. Use only for admin operations that legitimately need to read or write any row.  Role 
 student 
 Yoga teacher with verified credentials  wellness_practitioner 
 Staff — can manage most content, cannot access all admin features  admin 
Helper function in the database:
`sql
 Returns true if the current auth.uid() is admin or moderator
SELECT public.is_admin();
`
This function is used in RLS policies throughout the schema.
Checking Roles in Code
In a Server Component or Server Action:
`tsx
import { createSupabaseServerClient } from '@/lib/supabaseServer'
const supabase = await createSupabaseServerClient()
const { data: { user } } = await supabase.auth.getUser()
const { data: profile } = await supabase
  .from('profiles')
  .select('role')
  .eq('id', user!.id)
  .single()
const isAdmin = profile?.role === 'admin'
const isAdminOrMod = ['admin', 'moderator'].includes(profile?.role ?? '')
`
In a layout (authoritative gate pattern):
`tsx
// app/admin/layout.tsx
import { redirect } from 'next/navigation'
import { createSupabaseServerClient } from '@/lib/supabaseServer'
export default async function AdminLayout({ children }) {
  const supabase = await createSupabaseServerClient()
  const { data: { user } } = await supabase.auth.getUser()
  if (!user) redirect('/signin')
  const { data: profile } = await supabase
    .from('profiles').select('role').eq('id', user.id).single()
  if (!['admin', 'moderator'].includes(profile?.role ?? '')) {
    redirect('/')
  }
  return <AdminShell>{children}</AdminShell>
}
`
The middleware check is a first pass for UX. The layout check is the security gate — never rely on middleware alone for admin gating.
Admin Impersonation
Admins can view the platform as any member without knowing their password. The mechanism:
Admin visits /admin/users/[id] and clicks "Impersonate".
A Server Action writes a goya_impersonating cookie (value = target user ID) and a goya_impersonation_log_id cookie.
A log row is inserted into impersonation_log.
The admin's own session remains active — only the displayed data changes.
Server Components that render memberspecific data call getImpersonationState() from lib/impersonation.ts to resolve the effective user ID.
On "End Impersonation", both cookies are cleared and the log row is updated with ended_at.
Security: Middleware validates the impersonation cookie on every request. If the real session user's role is not admin, the cookies are deleted and the user is redirected.
`tsx
// lib/impersonation.ts — resolving the effective user
import { getImpersonationState } from '@/lib/impersonation'
const state = await getImpersonationState()
const effectiveUserId = state.isImpersonating
  ? state.targetUserId
  : currentUser.id
`
Migrated Users — Password Reset Gate
Members imported from the legacy WordPress platform have profiles.requires_password_reset = true. Middleware intercepts every request for these users and redirects to /account/setpassword until they set a new password, at which point the flag is cleared.
Maintenance Mode
Controlled via site_settings keys:
 Value 
 "true" 
 "true" 
 ISO datetime 
 ISO datetime 
During maintenance, all nonadmin users are redirected to /maintenance. Admins and moderators pass through.
The settings are fetched with a 60second modulelevel cache in middleware to avoid hitting the DB on every request.
See Also
architecture.md — Middleware and layoutlevel role checks
databaseschema.md — profiles table and impersonation_log`
deployment.md — Required environment variables for auth

title: Contributing
content:
Contributing This guide covers the conventions, workflows, and patterns you need to contribute to GOYA v2. Table of Contents Code Style Conventions CLAUDE.md Rules Adding a New Page Adding a New Admin Page Adding a Database Migration Testing Activity and Logging PR Process Code Style Conventions TypeScript Strict mode is enabled. No any without a comment explaining why. Use the generated types/supabase.ts for database types. Regenerate with npx supabase gen types typescript linked > types/supabase.ts after schema changes. Prefer explicit return types on exported functions. React / Next.js Server Components by default — only add 'use client' when you need interactivity or browser APIs. No useEffect for data fetching. Fetch in Server Components using await supabase.from(...). Server Actions ('use server') for all mutations (create, update, delete). Colocate actions.ts files next to the page that uses them. Imports Use the @/ path alias for all imports from the project root. Import order: external packages, then @/lib/..., then @/app/..., then relative. Styling Tailwind CSS only. No inline style props except for dynamic values that cannot be expressed as classes. Never hardcode maxwidth, mxauto, or horizontal padding on pagelevel elements — use PageContainer. Naming Files: kebabcase.tsx Components: PascalCase Functions and variables: camelCase Database columns: snake_case (Supabase convention) Environment variables: SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE CLAUDE.md Rules CLAUDE.md at the project root defines hard constraints that apply to all contributions: PageContainer is mandatory for all page content sections. Width must be maxw7xl via PageContainer. No exceptions. Log errors to LOG.md under "Open Issues" when you encounter unexpected behaviour. Activity files: After completing a quick task, create activity/quicktasks/quicktask_TaskName_DDMMYYYY.md. Milestone activity files: On milestone start or completion, create activity/vXXX_MilestoneName_DDMMYYYY.md and update activity/README.md. Always run npx supabase db push after creating a migration file. These are not suggestions. Reviewers will reject PRs that violate them. Adding a New Page Create app/yoursection/page.tsx. It is a Server Component by default. ``tsx // app/schools/page.tsx import { createSupabaseServerClient } from '@/lib/supabaseServer' import PageContainer from '@/app/components/ui/PageContainer' export default async function SchoolsPage() { const supabase = await createSupabaseServerClient() const { data: schools } = await supabase.from('schools').select('') return (

Schools

{/ render schools /} ) } ` If the page needs to be protected, add the path to PROTECTED_PATHS in middleware.ts. If the page has mutations, add app/yoursection/actions.ts with 'use server' functions. If the page has interactive UI, create Client Components in app/yoursection/components/. Adding a New Admin Page Create app/admin/yoursection/page.tsx. The app/admin/layout.tsx already handles role verification. Add a nav link to app/admin/components/AdminShell.tsx in the appropriate nav group. Follow the same Server Component + PageContainer pattern. `tsx // app/admin/yoursection/page.tsx import PageContainer from '@/app/components/ui/PageContainer' export default async function AdminYourSectionPage() { return (

Your Section

 ) } ` If the page needs API routes (e.g. for complex async operations), add app/api/admin/yoursection/route.ts. For simple mutations, use Server Actions. Adding a Database Migration Create a new file in supabase/migrations/ with the naming pattern YYYYMMDD_description.sql. Use today's date and increment if multiple migrations exist for the same date (e.g. 20260401_add_newsletter_table.sql). Write the SQL. Always include: ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; for new tables RLS policies for each relevant role An updated_at trigger if the table has an updated_at column Apply to your local/dev database: `bash npx supabase db push ` Update types/supabase.ts: `bash npx supabase gen types typescript linked > types/supabase.ts ` Commit both the migration file and the updated types. Never alter the database directly (Supabase Studio SQL editor or otherwise). All schema changes must be in migration files so they can be reproduced on any environment. Testing GOYA v2 uses Vitest with Testing Library. Run tests: `bash npx vitest npx vitest run # single pass (CI mode) npx vitest coverage # with coverage report ` Test file locations: __tests__/ — unit tests for lib/ utilities lib/stripe/handlers/.test.ts — colocated handler tests app/*.test.tsx — colocated component tests What to test: All lib/ utility functions, especially email, credits, and Stripe handlers Server Actions that contain nontrivial business logic API route handlers (use fetch mocking) What not to test: Simple Server Components that only render data — rely on type safety and integration tests Next.js routing behaviour — that's framework responsibility Mocking Supabase: Use vi.mock('@/lib/supabaseServer') and return a typed mock client. See existing tests in lib/stripe/handlers/ for patterns. Activity and Logging Error Log When you encounter an error or unexpected behaviour during development, append to LOG.md: ` [20260331] [ERROR] [admin/users] — Bulk delete fails when user has orphaned stripe_orders rows OPEN ` When resolved, move to the "Resolved" section and mark RESOLVED. Quick Task Activity After completing any /gsd:quick task or standalone piece of work, create: ` activity/quicktasks/quicktask_shortkebabdescription_DDMMYYYY.md ` Contents: task description, status, solution. One file per task. Milestone Activity On milestone start or completion, create: ` activity/vXXX_MilestoneName_DDMMYYYY.md ` And update the table in activity/README.md. PR Process Branch from develop (or main for hotfixes). Follow the naming convention: feat/shortdescription, fix/shortdescription, chore/shortdescription. Ensure npm run lint passes with no errors. Ensure npx vitest run passes. If the PR adds a new migration, confirm npx supabase db push succeeds on a clean environment. PRs that violate CLAUDE.md rules (missing PageContainer, hardcoded widths) will be rejected at review. Squash commits on merge to main`. See Also architecture.md — Folder structure and key patterns overview.md — Initial setup instructions databaseschema.md — Table reference for writing migrations

title: Deployment
content:
Deployment
GOYA v2 is deployed on Vercel, connected to the main branch. Database is hosted on Supabase.
Table of Contents
Vercel Deployment
Environment Variables
Database Migrations
Cron Jobs
Maintenance Mode
Stripe Webhook Registration
Vercel Deployment
The project deploys automatically on push to main. Preview deployments are created for PRs.
Manual deploy from CLI:
``bash
vercel prod
`
Build command: next build
Output directory: .next (standard Next.js)
Node.js version: 20.x
No special build configuration is required. The next.config.ts is standard.
Environment Variables
Set these in the Vercel project dashboard under Settings → Environment Variables, or in .env.local for local development.
Supabase
 Required 
 Yes 
 Yes 
 Yes 
Stripe
 Required 
 Yes 
 Yes 
Resend (Email)
 Required 
 Yes 
Secrets Store
 Required 
 Yes 
Map
 Required 
 Yes 
Cron Security
 Required 
 Yes 
AI / Chatbot
 Conditional 
 If using Claude 
 If using OpenAI 
AI provider selection is controlled via site_settings in the database (ai_provider key), so switching providers does not require a redeploy.
Analytics
 Optional 
 Optional 
Database Migrations
Migrations are applied manually using the Supabase CLI. They are not automatically applied on deploy.
After merging code that contains new migration files:
`bash
npx supabase db push
`
This applies all pending migrations in supabase/migrations/ to the linked Supabase project.
Link a new environment:
`bash
npx supabase link projectref yourprojectref
`
Cron Jobs
Cron jobs are defined in vercel.json and executed by Vercel's builtin scheduler. Each job calls an API route handler with a CRON_SECRET header for verification.
 Schedule 
 Daily at 08:00 UTC 
 Mondays at 08:00 UTC 
 Every 5 minutes 
 Daily at 03:00 UTC 
Testing cron routes locally:
`bash
curl H "xcronsecret: yourcronsecret" http://localhost:3000/api/cron/creditsexpiring
`
Maintenance Mode
Maintenance mode is controlled from the admin panel at /admin/settings, not from environment variables or code. Two modes are available:
Immediate: Activate now — all nonadmin users see the /maintenance splash page.
Scheduled: Set a UTC start and end time — maintenance activates and deactivates automatically.
The middleware fetches these settings from site_settings with a 60second cache, so activation propagates within one minute.
Admins and moderators are never blocked by maintenance mode.
Stripe Webhook Registration
For production, register the webhook endpoint in the Stripe Dashboard:
Go to Stripe Dashboard → Developers → Webhooks.
Add endpoint: https://yourdomain.com/api/webhooks/stripe
Select the following events to listen for:
product.created, product.updated, product.deleted
price.created, price.updated, price.deleted
coupon.created, coupon.updated, coupon.deleted
customer.subscription.created, customer.subscription.updated, customer.subscription.deleted
payment_intent.succeeded, payment_intent.payment_failed
invoice.paid, invoice.payment_failed
checkout.session.completed
Copy the signing secret (whsec_...) into the STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET environment variable.
For local development, use the Stripe CLI to forward events:
`bash
stripe listen forwardto localhost:3000/api/webhooks/stripe
`
The CLI will print a local webhook secret — use it as STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET in .env.local`.
See Also
stripeintegration.md — How the webhook endpoint works
authentication.md — Supabase auth environment variables
emailsystem.md — Resend configuration

title: Email System
content:
Email System GOYA v2 sends transactional email via Resend. Templates are stored in the database and editable by admins without a code deploy. All sends are logged to email_log. Table of Contents Architecture Overview Resend Client Sending Email sendEmailFromTemplate (preferred) sendEmail (legacy) Template System DB Templates Variable Substitution HTML Layout Wrapper Fallback Defaults Email Sandbox (dev/staging) Email Log Adding a New Email Template Architecture Overview `` Server Action / Route Handler │ ▼ sendEmailFromTemplate() ← lib/email/send.ts │ ├── Fetch template from DB (email_templates table) ├── Substitute {{variables}} in subject + content ├── Wrap content in wrapInEmailLayout() ├── Apply sandbox redirect (if enabled) └── resend.emails.send() │ ├── Log result to email_log table └── Return { success, reason?, error? } ` Resend Client lib/email/client.ts exports a lazyinitialised Resend singleton. The client is only instantiated on first call, so a missing RESEND_API_KEY does not crash the build — it throws at send time. `ts FROM_ADDRESS = 'hello@globalonlineyogaassociation.org' REPLY_TO = 'member@globalonlineyogaassociation.org' ` Sending Email sendEmailFromTemplate (preferred) Use this for all new code. It reads the template from the database, performs variable substitution, wraps the HTML, and sends via Resend. `ts import { sendEmailFromTemplate } from '@/lib/email/send' await sendEmailFromTemplate({ to: 'member@example.com', templateKey: 'welcome_member', variables: { first_name: 'Priya', login_url: 'https://app.goya.org/signin', }, }) ` Return value: `ts { success: boolean reason?: 'template_inactive' // Template not found or disabled — silently skipped error?: unknown // Resend API or render error } ` If template_inactive is returned, no error is logged and no exception is thrown. This is intentional — disabled templates should fail silently. sendEmail (legacy) @deprecated — accepts a React Email template prop. Do not use for new templates. It still works but bypasses the DBdriven template system. Template System DB Templates Templates live in the email_templates table: Description Unique string identifier e.g. welcome_member, verification_approved subject HTML body content — supports {{variables}} is_active Templates are managed from the admin panel at /admin/emailtemplates. Variable Substitution Both subject and html_content support {{variable_name}} placeholders. These are replaced by the variables object passed to sendEmailFromTemplate. `html

Hi {{first_name}},

Your verification has been approved. Sign in

` Any {{placeholder}} that has no matching key in variables is stripped (replaced with empty string) before sending. HTML Layout Wrapper lib/email/wrapper.ts exports wrapInEmailLayout(content: string): string. It wraps any HTML fragment in a full email document with: GOYA branded header (dark navy #0f2044 background with wordmark) White content card (600px maxwidth, responsive) Footer with copyright, contact email, Privacy Policy, Terms of Use, and Unsubscribe links sendEmailFromTemplate automatically wraps content before sending. You do not need to call wrapInEmailLayout manually. Fallback Defaults lib/email/defaults.ts exports DEFAULT_TEMPLATES — a record of templateKey → { content: string }. If a template row exists in the DB but html_content is empty, the fallback content is used. This allows templates to exist in the DB (for admin editing) before content has been written. Email Sandbox (dev/staging) To avoid sending real emails during development or QA, the email sandbox intercepts all outgoing sends and redirects them to a configured address. Controlled via site_settings: Value "true" to activate email_sandbox_recipient When active, every email is redirected and the subject is prefixed with [SANDBOX → original@address.com]. The sandbox state is cached for 30 seconds to avoid repeated DB queries. Set from the admin panel at /admin/settings. Email Log Every send attempt (success or failure) is recorded in email_log: Value Original intended recipient (before sandbox) subject templateKey or legacy template name status Populated on failure Log is viewable from /admin/emailtemplates (email log tab). Adding a New Email Template Insert a row into email_templates via a new migration file or the admin panel: `sql INSERT INTO email_templates (template_key, subject, html_content, is_active) VALUES ( 'course_enrollment_confirmed', 'You''re enrolled in {{course_title}}', '

Hi {{first_name}},

You''ve been enrolled in {{course_title}}.

', true ); ` Add a fallback in lib/email/defaults.ts (optional but recommended): `ts export const DEFAULT_TEMPLATES: Record = { course_enrollment_confirmed: { content: '

You have been enrolled in a new course.

', }, // ... } ` Send the email from your Server Action or route handler: `ts await sendEmailFromTemplate({ to: user.email, templateKey: 'course_enrollment_confirmed', variables: { first_name: profile.full_name ?? 'Member', course_title: course.title, }, }) ` Test by enabling the email sandbox in dev settings and confirming the send in email log. See Also databaseschema.md — email_templates and email_log table schemas deployment.md — RESEND_API_KEY` environment variable contributing.md — When to use Server Actions vs route handlers

title: Developer Guide Overview
content:
Developer Guide Overview
This guide covers everything you need to build, run, and contribute to GOYA v2 — the platform for the Global Online Yoga Association.
Table of Contents
Tech Stack
Prerequisites
Getting Started
Project Structure at a Glance
Key Conventions
Tech Stack
 Technology 
 Next.js App Router 
 TypeScript 
 Tailwind CSS 
 Supabase (PostgreSQL + RLS) 
 Supabase Storage 
 Resend 
 Stripe 
 Vercel 
 Anthropic Claude SDK + OpenAI 
 Vitest + Testing Library 
 Tiptap 
 Recharts 
 Mapbox GL 
Prerequisites
Node.js 20+
A Supabase project (with service role key)
A Stripe account (with webhook endpoint configured)
A Resend account and verified sending domain
Vercel CLI (optional, for production deploys)
Getting Started
Clone and install
``bash
git clone <repourl>
cd "GOYA v2"
npm install
`
Set up environment variables
Copy the example file and fill in your credentials:
`bash
cp .env.example .env.local
`
Required variables:
`env
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=https://yourproject.supabase.co
NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=youranonkey
SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY=yourservicerolekey
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_test_...
STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET=whsec_...
RESEND_API_KEY=re_...
SECRETS_MASTER_KEY=a32bytehexstring
NEXT_PUBLIC_MAPBOX_TOKEN=pk.eyJ1...
`
See deployment.md for the full variable reference.
Apply database migrations
`bash
npx supabase db push
`
Always run this after pulling changes that add new migration files.
Start the dev server
`bash
npm run dev
`
The app runs at http://localhost:3000.
Lint
`bash
npm run lint
`
Project Structure at a Glance
`
app/          Next.js App Router pages + API routes
lib/          Shared serverside utilities (Supabase, Stripe, email, etc.)
supabase/     Database migrations and schema
types/        Generated Supabase TypeScript types
public/       Static assets
__tests__     Vitest unit tests
`
See architecture.md for the full annotated directory tree.
Key Conventions
Server Components by default. Only add 'use client' when you need interactivity, browser APIs, or React hooks.
Server Actions for mutations. Form submissions and data writes use Server Actions ('use server'), not clientside fetch to API routes.
PageContainer for layout width. Every page content section must use app/components/ui/PageContainer.tsx to stay aligned with the header and footer. Never hardcode maxwidth or horizontal padding on pages.
Supabase migrations only. All schema changes go through supabase/migrations/. Never modify the database manually.
Run npx supabase db push` after every migration file.
See Also
architecture.md — Annotated folder tree and key patterns
databaseschema.md — All tables and RLS policies
authentication.md — Auth flow and role system
contributing.md — Code conventions and PR process

title: Storage
content: Storage GOYA v2 uses Supabase Storage for all useruploaded and adminuploaded files. Each category of file lives in its own bucket with purposespecific RLS policies. Table of Contents Buckets Avatar Uploads Event Images School Logos Upgrade Certificates Chatbot Avatars Post Media (Community Feed) Migration Uploads Media Library Buckets Accessing Storage URLs Buckets Public Yes Yes Yes No Yes Yes Yes Yes No All buckets are defined via SQL migrations in supabase/migrations/. RLS policies on storage.objects control who can read, insert, update, and delete. Avatar Uploads Route handler: app/api/avatar/route.ts The avatar upload flow: The client POSTs the image file as multipart/formdata to /api/avatar. The route handler authenticates the user via the server Supabase client. The file is uploaded to the avatars bucket at path {userId}/avatar.{ext}. The route handler updates profiles.avatar_url with the new public URL. The URL is returned to the client. Storage path pattern: avatars/{user_id}/avatar.{extension} The avatars bucket is public, so URLs can be used directly in or next/image without signed tokens. Event Images Bucket: eventimages (public) Uploaded by admins/moderators from the admin event editor. The public URL is stored in events.featured_image_url. RLS: Public: SELECT Admin/moderator: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE School Logos Bucket: schoollogos (public) Uploaded by admins from the schools admin section. Stored in schools.logo_url. RLS: Public: SELECT Admin only: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE Upgrade Certificates Bucket: upgradecertificates (private) Used during the WP (Wellness Practitioner) upgrade flow. Members upload proof documents (PDF or image) as part of their designation application. Files are read by admins during the review process. Because this bucket is private, access requires a signed URL or the service role client. Chatbot Avatars Bucket: chatbotavatars (public) Adminuploaded avatar image for the GOYA chatbot. URL stored in site_settings under the key chatbot_avatar_url. Post Media (Community Feed) Buckets: postimages, postvideos, postaudio (all public) Community feed posts can include attached media. Files are uploaded clientside and the public URL is stored with the post record. Migration Uploads Bucket: migrationuploads (private, admin only) Temporary staging area for the WordPress member import tool. The admin uploads a JSON export file, which the /api/admin/migration/import route handler downloads from storage, processes, then deletes. File size limit: 50 MB. Allowed MIME types: application/json only. Media Library Buckets The media library (/admin/media) is a centralised file manager that provides a UI for browsing and organising files across multiple buckets. The media_items table stores metadata for every catalogued file. Buckets managed through the media library (defined in app/admin/media/constants.ts): Label in UI Avatars eventimages Courses upgradecertificates Uploads Backfill Script Because media_items was added after files already existed in storage, a onetime backfill script is provided: ``bash npm run media:backfill ` Source: scripts/backfillmediaitems.ts. The script is idempotent — safe to rerun; it skips files already registered in media_items. New file uploads after the initial backfill are registered automatically via lib/media/register.ts. See databaseschema.md for the media_items and media_folders table schemas. Accessing Storage URLs Public buckets — construct the URL directly: `ts const url = ${process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL}/storage/v1/object/public/${bucket}/${path} ` Supabase Storage also returns the public URL from the upload response: `ts const { data } = await supabase.storage .from('avatars') .upload(path, file, { upsert: true }) const { data: { publicUrl } } = supabase.storage .from('avatars') .getPublicUrl(data.path) ` Private buckets — generate a signed URL using the service role client: `ts import { getSupabaseService } from '@/lib/supabase/service' const supabase = getSupabaseService() const { data } = await supabase.storage .from('upgradecertificates') .createSignedUrl(filePath, 3600) // 1 hour expiry ` When displaying avatars or event images, prefer next/image with the public URL for automatic optimisation. See Also databaseschema.md — media_items and media_folders tables architecture.md — Where avatar upload route lives in app/api/` deployment.md — Storagerelated environment variables

title: Stripe Integration
content:
Stripe Integration
GOYA v2 uses Stripe for membership subscriptions, onetime purchases, and coupon management. The integration follows a writepartitioning pattern: Stripe is the source of truth, and a set of mirror tables in Supabase act as a local cache for fast querying.
Table of Contents
Stripe SDK Singleton
Webhook Endpoint
HMAC Verification
Idempotency Gate
Event Dispatch
Event Handlers
Mirror Tables
WritePartitioning Pattern
Cron: Pending Events
Admin Stripe Sync
Stripe SDK Singleton
lib/stripe/client.ts exports a lazy getStripe() function. It imports serveronly to prevent the Stripe SDK (and secret key) from ever reaching the browser bundle.
``ts
import 'serveronly'
import Stripe from 'stripe'
export function getStripe(): Stripe {
  // initialised once, reused across requests in the same server instance
}
`
Configuration: maxNetworkRetries: 3, timeout: 10000ms.
Never import getStripe() from a Client Component. TypeScript will catch this via the serveronly import.
Webhook Endpoint
Route: POST /api/webhooks/stripe
This is the single entry point for all Stripe events. It is intentionally not under /api/v1/ — it uses Stripe's own HMAC signature for authentication, not the GOYA API key system.
HMAC Verification
The raw request body (as text) and the stripesignature header are passed to:
`ts
event = getStripe().webhooks.constructEvent(body, signature, process.env.STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET)
`
If signature verification fails, the handler returns 400 immediately. Never read request.json() before calling constructEvent — it must receive the raw body string.
Idempotency Gate
Before processing, the handler inserts a row into webhook_events with status: 'processing':
`ts
await supabase.from('webhook_events').insert({
  stripe_event_id: event.id,
  event_type: event.type,
  status: 'processing',
  payload: event,
})
`
If the insert returns a unique constraint violation (code: '23505'), the event is a duplicate — return 200 immediately without processing. This handles Stripe's atleastonce delivery guarantee.
Event Dispatch
After the idempotency insert, the event is dispatched to the appropriate handler via a switch on event.type. The handler's return value determines the final webhook_events.status:
 Final Status 
 processed 
 pending_cron 
 failed 
Stripe requires a 200 response even for events that fail processing — otherwise Stripe retries endlessly. Failures are handled asynchronously via the admin panel and cron.
Event Handlers
One file per Stripe object type in lib/stripe/handlers/:
 Events Handled 
 product.created, product.updated, product.deleted  price.ts 
 coupon.created, coupon.updated, coupon.deleted  subscription.ts 
 payment_intent.succeeded, .payment_failed  invoice.ts 
 checkout.session.completed  Table  Key Column  stripe_products  stripe_id (Stripe prod_...)  stripe_prices  stripe_id (Stripe price_...)  stripe_orders  stripe_id  stripe_coupons  stripe_coupon_id  stripe_coupon_redemptions  Appendonly 
No explicit FK constraints between mirror tables. Stripe webhooks can arrive out of order (e.g. a price event before its product event). FKs on text stripe_id columns would cause constraint violations for legitimate outoforder delivery.
All mirror tables have an updated_at column maintained by a BEFORE UPDATE trigger, except stripe_coupon_redemptions which is appendonly.
WritePartitioning Pattern
Stripe = source of truth. Supabase = read cache.
Never write prices or subscription status directly to the Supabase mirror tables from application code.
Always mutate via Stripe API (cancel subscription, apply coupon, etc.).
The webhook will fire and update the mirror tables automatically.
This ensures the mirror is always consistent with Stripe and avoids race conditions from direct DB writes.
Reading for UI: Query stripe_orders and stripe_products directly for fast display. No Stripe API call needed for display.
Example: checking a user's active subscription:
`ts
const { data: order } = await supabase
  .from('stripe_orders')
  .select('subscription_status, current_period_end, cancel_at_period_end')
  .eq('user_id', userId)
  .eq('type', 'recurring')
  .order('created_at', { ascending: false })
  .limit(1)
  .single()
`
Cron: Pending Events
Route: GET /api/cron/stripeevents (runs every 5 minutes via Vercel cron)
Some events are set to pending_cron status when the handler cannot complete synchronously (e.g. a dependency hasn't been mirrored yet due to outoforder delivery). The cron job retries these events.
The cron is protected by CRON_SECRET header validation.
Admin Stripe Sync
Route: POST /api/admin/stripesync
A manual fullsync endpoint that pulls the current state of products, prices, and active subscriptions from the Stripe API and upserts them into the mirror tables. Used after initial setup or to recover from extended webhook downtime.
Only accessible to admins. Not exposed in the public API.
See Also
databaseschema.md — Mirror table schemas and webhook_events
deployment.md — STRIPE_SECRET_KEY and STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET env vars
apireference.md — The /api/v1/webhooks/payment` external webhook endpoint (separate from the Stripe webhook)
